1.1: The hearth — a stone fireplace wide enough to roast in, black with age. The mantel holds a cracked bell and a coil of rope. Its light flickers across the room and throws the corner table into shadow; whoever faces it is dazzled to the dark beyond.
1.2: The bar — dark oak, scarred and ale-polished, eight stools. Behind it: shelved bottles, a rack of cups, a trap to the cellar. Bram stands a half-step up, with a clear view of the whole room and the door.
1.3: The corner table — behind the settle-back, candle-lit, seats four. The settle blocks the sight of the door; whoever sits here sees the hearth and half the room but not who enters until they round the screen. Most nights it is held by a hooded woman the regulars call Maren — still, watchful, a cup she rarely drinks. She says little and gives nothing to idle curiosity, but she marks everyone who comes in asking about the bone-charms or the missing; to one who asks plainly and means it she will offer, in few words, to walk them up a marked deer-path herself and show them what has been taken. Her price is seriousness; her patience for anything else is short.
1.4: The lane door — heavy oak, iron-studded, opens inward on the muddy square. When it opens the cold and the forest-smell come in, the draught reaches the hearth, and heads at the bar turn.
1.5: The common floor — packed earth and board, five tables scattered without order, two or three taken on an ordinary night. The open stretch between bar and hearth is the way through; anyone crossing it is seen from both.

1.1: The Beaten Drum's main room. The quarter's only common house. Open to anyone with coin.
1.2: Bram keeps the bar — broad, grey, unhurried, twenty years behind this counter. He makes first contact with strangers, not the patrons. Drawing a blade indoors loses your welcome, and the Drum is the only warmth in the quarter.

3.1: The figure's stillness is the stillness of listening, not absence.
3._: The settle-back screens the corner table from the door; Bram seats travellers there who want to go unseen. A hooded figure has held that seat since dusk, nursing one cup.

1._: What is known in the main room.

_._: The main room — low oak beams, a wide stone hearth on the long wall, a bar of dark scarred timber down the right side. Five mismatched tables on a floor of packed earth and worn board. The lane door is heavy and studded, in full view of the bar but screened from the corner table by a high settle-back. Tallow lamps hang from the beams; the fire is always lit. It smells of woodsmoke, spilled ale, and wet wool. At the corner table, behind the settle-back, a hooded figure sits unmoving, the hood forward over a cup gone cold. At one of the nearer tables a broad-shouldered man and a travel-worn woman sit with their heads close, talking low.

2.1: The range — banked coals, a pot of stew kept warm, a rack of knives within easy reach.
2.2: The back door — opens on the midden and the lane behind; bolted at night.
2._: The kitchen — behind a curtain at the back of the bar. A range, a long table, hanging bunches of herb and onion. Not public; a stranger pushing through the curtain is noticed at once.

1.3: The loft room — up a ladder-stair from behind the bar, a single low room let to travellers. A pallet, a stool, a shuttered window over the square. The stair creaks; anyone climbing it is heard at the bar.
1._: The Beaten Drum — the quarter's tavern, a low timber-and-daub building with a sagging slate roof and a faded sign of a split war-drum. Two rooms below, a loft above. Warm, smoke-dark, the first shelter a traveller reaches coming out of Thornwood.

1.2: The smithy — across the square, cold at this hour, the forge banked. Hanlon the smith drinks at the Drum most nights. (Stub — extend by subnest when play moves here.)
1.3: The market stalls — a chandler's and a fishmonger's under a leaking awning, shuttered after dark. (Stub — extend by subnest when play moves here.)
1._: The market quarter — Oakhollow's lower end, where the Thornwood road meets the town. Mud and cobble, woodsmoke, the smell of the forest close by. A few buildings cluster around a muddy square: the Beaten Drum, a smithy, a covered stall or two. Travellers off the Thornwood road arrive here first.

1.1: The watching has a direction: it comes down from upslope, north, where the trunks stand thickest and blackest. Whatever holds this path in its attention sits that way.
1.2: The bone-charm at the marker behind them was hung within the hour and turned to face inward — not warding this path but turning travellers onto it. Someone meant them to walk here.
1.3: A hand's breadth west of the trail, under the rot, the old deer-craft still runs true: a clean line the corruption has not touched, faint and cold, there for one who can read the ground.
1._: A deer-trail thinning to nothing underfoot, a few hundred paces in from the charcoal marker. The trees crowd close and the canopy shuts out the sky; the dark is near total. The quiet here has weight — not the absence of sound but the presence of attention. The ground is faintly wrong underfoot, a cold that is not only the night.

2.1: Off to the east, a few paces through the trees, the watched path runs — and on it tonight, three who are not foresters: the snap of a careful boot, the small sounds of people who do not quite belong to the wood.
2.2: The watching that holds the eastern path does not reach here; this line runs below its attention. To stay on it is to stay unseen.
2._: A faint trail a little west of the deer-path, so slight it is more the memory of a path than a path — the old deer-craft line, clean ground the corruption has not reached. Cold and quiet, but an honest quiet. The trees stand a little apart here; a careful traveller moves fast and unheard.

1.3: The charcoal-burner's clearing — where the deer-paths knot together a half-mile north of the marker, and the canopy breaks to a ragged disc of grey sky. The burner's turf-stacks have fallen in and his hearths gone cold years since; nothing has worked this ground since the rot came down from Thornwall. The bone-charms hang thickest here, a dozen strung from the low branches, and every one turned to face inward — south, down the paths, the way a hand turns a charm not to ward a place but to herd what leaves it. North of the clearing the earth goes black and the cold deepens past the night's cold; the quiet there is the quiet of something holding its breath. To one who reads the deer-craft this is plainly the threshold — south of it the wood can still be walked; past it the wood no longer belongs to itself.
1._: The deer-paths — a web of narrow trails worn through the undergrowth, branching and rejoining, legible to those who know them and a maze to those who do not. The charcoal marker at the wood's edge is where they begin; bone-charms hang at the forks. Some paths run clean; some cross ground the corruption has touched, where the quiet goes wrong and the trees crowd black.

2._: Thornwood — the old forest north and west of Oakhollow, its edge a half-day from the Beaten Drum. By day a working wood of deer-paths and charcoal clearings; by night its own country, where what moves is not always game. A sickness has worked south and west from past Thornwall on the north side; the deer-paths nearest it are hung with bone-charms to ward the ground. Travellers keep to the marked paths and are off them by dark.

_._: Spatial map of Oakhollow at the edge of Thornwood. Floor 3: top-level positions are pscale 2 (district), their children pscale 1 (buildings), their children pscale 0 (rooms, human scale), and below the floor the fixtures (-1), seats (-2), and held things (-3). A three-digit address lands in a room. Author by subnest (finer detail within a place) and supernest (a coarser layer — the wider region — wrapped above); existing addresses absorb under leading-zero padding.
